chemicals.chemical_formula: Not provided in the given sources.
chemicals.concentration_notes: ◦ Concentration: The toxins are highly concentrated in the green tubers, the sprouts (eyes), and the peels of the potato5. Conversely, cooked, peeled potatoes contain only trace amounts of the toxins and are considered tolerable for pets in moderation5. ◦ Feline Sensitivity: Cats are notably more sensitive to solanine than dogs67. Because cats lack a specific liver detoxification process called gl
chemicals.description: in cats: These glycoalkaloids act as both cytotoxins and neurotoxins, affecting the cat's body through three primary mechanisms: ◦ Cytotoxic effect on cell membranes: The toxins interact with the phospholipids of cell membranes, increasing their permeability and causing the cells to be destroyed2. This particularly damages the epithelial cells of the intestines, leading to severe gastrointestinal
chemicals.name: s: Solanine and α-chaconine

description: Plain cooked potato flesh is not toxic to cats, but all green parts of the potato plant (leaves, stems, sprouts, and green-tinged skin) contain solanine — a glycoalkaloid found across the nightshade family that causes GI and neurological toxicity. Solanine levels decrease as the potato ripens; fully ripe, non-green potato flesh is safe in small amounts.
name: Potato
safetyNotes: Avoid: green potatoes, potato sprouts, and all green plant parts — these contain toxic solanine concentrations
Safe: plain cooked potato flesh (boiled or baked, without skin or seasonings) is not toxic in small amounts
Symptoms if green parts ingested: vomiting, diarrhea, lethargy, weakness, CNS depression in severe cases
Never feed seasoned, salted, buttered, or fried potato products — seasonings pose additional risks
Potato chips and crisps are a separate concern due to their extremely high salt content

symptoms.name: Hypersalivation (drooling), vomiting, diarrhea (which may be bloody), and severe abdominal pain
symptoms.notes: Solanine acts as a strong mucous membrane irritant; it interacts with the cell membranes' phospholipids, destroying the intestinal epithelial cells35. Cats are exceptionally sensitive to these toxins due to their limited liver detoxification capacity (lack of glucuronidation), meaning even small amo
symptoms.onset: Initial symptoms typically appear as early as 2 to 6 hours after ingestion

symptoms.name: Lethargy, apathy, muscle tremors, ataxia (loss of coordination), stupor, paralysis, and seizures
symptoms.notes: These neurological symptoms occur because solanine inhibits acetylcholinesterase in the brain, leading to a dangerous overstimulation of the parasympathetic nervous system36. The toxins also disrupt the cells' energy balance and mitochondrial function, contributing to profound weakness and lethargy7
symptoms.onset: Usually develops after the initial gastrointestinal symptoms, within 2 to 24 hours post-ingestion

symptoms.name: Bradycardia (abnormally slow heart rate) and circulatory failure
symptoms.notes: Cardiovascular symptoms generally manifest when the cat has been exposed to high doses of the toxin2. 4. Red Blood Cell Destruction and Kidney Damage
symptoms.onset: Develops progressively as the toxin is absorbed,

symptoms.name: Hemolysis (destruction of red blood cells) leading to anemia, hemoglobinuria (dark red to brown urine), icterus
symptoms.notes: In severe poisoning cases, the destruction of red blood cells releases hemoglobin into the bloodstream. This free hemoglobin has a nephrotoxic effect, meaning it damages the kidneys and directly contributes to acute kidney failure38. 5. Respiratory Depression
symptoms.onset: Delayed; the full extent of these signs often develops 12 to 24 hours after ingestion

symptoms.name: Difficulty breathing and respiratory paralysis
symptoms.notes: In the most severe cases of nightshade (potato) poisoning, death ultimately occurs due to a combination of respiratory paralysis and circulatory failure
symptoms.onset: Develops in the later, critical stages of poisoning, typically 12 to 24 hours post-exposure

treatments.description: The veterinarian will medically induce vomiting (using medications like xylazine for cats) to empty the stomach of the plant material. Alternatively, they may perform a gastric lavage (stomach pump) while the cat is under anesthesia
treatments.name: Emesis Induction or Gastric Lavage
treatments.notes: This is the most urgent step to prevent the toxins from being absorbed into the bloodstream, but it is primarily indicated if the ingestion was recent (within 1 to 2 hours) and the cat is currently asymptomatic or showing only mild symptoms1. 2. Toxin Binding

treatments.description: The vet will administer a dose of activated charcoal (1–4 g/kg of body weight) to bind to any unabsorbed solanine remaining in the cat's gastrointestinal tract
treatments.name: Activated Charcoal Administration
treatments.notes: In cases of severe poisoning, this administration may need to be repeated multiple times at 4 to 6-hour intervals1. 3. Intravenous (IV) Fluid Therapy

treatments.description: Administering fluids directly into the bloodstream to stimulate urine production (diuresis), promote the excretion of toxins, and treat dehydration caused by severe vomiting and diarrhea
treatments.name: Intravenous (IV) Fluid Therapy (Crystalloid Solutions)
treatments.notes: If the cat is exhibiting signs of red blood cell destruction (hemolysis), the IV infusion rate must be actively increased to ensure adequate blood flow to the kidneys and prevent acute kidney damage2. 4. Respiratory and Cardiovascular Emergency Support

treatments.description: Providing the cat with oxygen-enriched air, using a mechanical ventilator to assist with breathing, or using a defibrillator to correct life-threatening heart rhythms
treatments.name: Oxygen Supplementation, Mechanical Ventilation, and Defibrillation
treatments.notes: This level of intensive care is required in critical cases where the plant's toxins have caused severe respiratory depression, respiratory paralysis, or ventricular fibrillation of the heart23. 5. Neurological and Seizure Management

treatments.description: Administering medications such as benzodiazepines or barbiturates to control active muscle tremors and seizures
treatments.name: Anticonvulsant Therapy
treatments.notes: Indicated if the solanine toxin has advanced to cause severe central nervous system (CNS) overstimulation2. 6. Specific Symptomatic Medication

treatments.description: Administering the medication atropine to block specific nerve receptors
treatments.name: Atropine Administration
treatments.notes: While there is no direct, specific antidote for solanine poisoning, atropine is recommended to help partially compensate for and treat cholinergic symptoms (such as a dangerously slow heart rate or excessive salivation) caused by the toxin's effect on the brain
